perturbation.original_text: Any notice mailed as aforesaid shall be deemed to have been received by and given to the addressee on the fifth Business Day following the date of mailing except in the event of a disruption of postal service, in which event notice shall be delivered personally or given by telex, telegram or telecopy.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice mailed as aforesaid shall be deemed to have been received by and given to the addressee on the tenth Business Day following the date of mailing except in the event of a disruption of postal service, in which event notice shall be delivered personally or given by telex, telegram or telecopy.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the mail receipt timeframe to 10 business days may violate reasonable notice periods required by law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: British Columbia's Electronic Transactions Act, Section 24
perturbation.law_explanation: While BC's Electronic Transactions Act doesn't explicitly define mail receipt times, it establishes principles for determining when electronic documents are sent and received, influencing what is considered reasonable notice. Setting a 10-day receipt timeframe for mailed notices, especially when faster electronic options are available, could be deemed unreasonable or insufficient under legal scrutiny, especially if it prejudices a party's rights or obligations.

Electronic Transactions Act [SBC 2001] CHAPTER 10 Assented to April 11, 2001 Contents Part 1 — Definitions, Application and Exceptions 1 Definitions 2 Application and exceptions Part 2 — General 3 Legal recognition 4 Use not mandatory 5 Requirement for a record to be in writing 6 Requirement to provide information or a record to be in writing 7 Requirement to provide information or a record in a specified non-electronic form 8 Requirement to provide and retain originals 9 Requirement to retain a record 10 Whether record is capable of being retained 11 Signatures 12 Copies 13 Collection, storage, etc. 14 Electronic payments to or by the government of British Columbia Part 3 — Electronic Communication of Information and Records 15 Formation and operation of contracts 16 Involvement of electronic agents 17 Errors that occur while dealing with electronic agents 18 Sending or receiving information and records Part 4 — Carriage of Goods 19 Activity related to contracts of carriage of goods 20 Records Part 5 — Regulations 21 Power to make regulations 22 Commencement Part 1 — Definitions, Application and Exceptions Definitions 1 In this Act: "electronic" means created, recorded, transmitted or stored in digital or other intangible form by electronic, magnetic or optical means or by any other similar means; "electronic agent" means a computer program, or other electronic means, used to initiate an activity or to respond to electronic information, records or activities in whole or in part without review by an individual at the time of the response or activity; "electronic signature" means information in electronic form that a person has created or adopted in order to sign a record and that is in, attached to or associated with the record. Application and exceptions 2 (1) This Act does not limit the operation of a law that (a) expressly authorizes, prohibits or regulates the use of information or records in electronic form, or (b) requires information or a record to be posted, displayed or delivered in a specific
perturbation.justification: The modification from 5 to 10 business days for deemed receipt of notice introduces a potential contradiction regarding reasonable notice, as highlighted in the Perturbation and Law Explanations. While the Electronic Transactions Act (ETA) snippet provided doesn't specify mail receipt times, it emphasizes the importance of electronic communication and sets a precedent for reasonable standards in electronic transactions. By extending the receipt timeframe to 10 days, the changed text could be deemed unreasonable, particularly when faster electronic methods are available. The Perturbation Explanation specifically points out the potential violation of reasonable notice periods, while the Law Explanation suggests that such an extension could prejudice a party's rights, thus creating a legal contradiction under the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, as the time-sensitive obligation may not align with legal expectations of reasonable notification.

perturbation.original_text: All such representations and warranties shall not merge in or be prejudiced by, and shall survive for a period of three years from the completion of the distribution of the Units.
perturbation.changed_text: All such representations and warranties shall not merge in or be prejudiced by, and shall survive for a period of one year from the completion of the distribution of the Units.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the survival period of representations and warranties to one year may be insufficient, potentially conflicting with statutes of limitations for certain claims under British Columbia law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The British Columbia Limitations Act generally sets a basic limitation period of two years. Reducing the survival of representations and warranties to one year could prevent parties from pursuing legitimate claims if breaches are discovered after the one-year contractual period but within the statutory two-year limitation period.

Reg. 80/2013).] Copyright © King's Printer, Victoria, British Columbia, Canada
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the survival period of representations and warranties to one year, while the Law Explanation indicates that the British Columbia Limitations Act generally sets a basic limitation period of two years. If breaches are discovered after the one-year contractual period but within the statutory two-year limitation period, legitimate claims could be prevented. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that a Limitation Act existed in British Columbia, which was later repealed. While it doesn't provide the specific limitations period, the Perturbation Explanation says that reducing the period to one year "may be insufficient, potentially conflicting with statutes of limitations for certain claims under British Columbia law." The modification creates an inconsistency between the contractual term and the implied statutory limitation period, creating a contradiction because the contract sets a deadline that violates the presumed law.
